title: Individual Notification — Letter Generation & Mailing Evidence
exclusions: Excludes test/sandbox environments, pre-production systems, development instances without regulated data, and any systems formally documented as out-of-scope in the HIPAA Security programme scope statement approved by the control owner.
evidence: Saved search results archived to the `audit_evidence` index with HIPAA Security control tagging; scheduled PDF/CSV exports for auditor consumption; Splunk dashboard screenshots with timestamp watermarks capturing control operating effectiveness over the review period.

compliance.assurance_rationale: HIPAA Security §164.404(b) — Reviewed: SPL provides contributing evidence for this regulatory requirement. The search query directly addresses the monitoring objective specified by the clause.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that HIPAA Security §164.404(b) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.10.45: Individual Notification — Letter Generation & Mailing Evidence.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.10.45' running on index=privacy sourcetype=hipaa:breach_notice (incident_id, patient_token, letter_status, mail_tracking_id, mailed_dttm), archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

controlTest.positiveScenario: Inject a synthetic test event that satisfies the detection criteria for 'Individual Notification — Letter Generation & Mailing Eviden' — for example, create a log entry with all required fields populated to match the SPL filter conditions. Verify the search fires within the expected schedule interval, the alert populates the compliance dashboard, and the event routes correctly to the evidence index with proper HIPAA Security control tagging.
controlTest.negativeScenario: Ingest a batch of test events that are structurally similar but explicitly do NOT meet the detection threshold — e.g., events with compliant values, timestamps outside the monitoring window, or source systems excluded by the scope filter. Verify the search produces zero results, no false-positive alerts fire, and the compliance posture score remains unaffected for the HIPAA Security control objective.

app: HTTP Event Collector (HEC), Splunk Add-on for ServiceNow (Splunkbase 1928) if tickets drive mailhouse
spl:
index=privacy sourcetype="hipaa:breach_notice" earliest=-120d
| eval mailed_epoch=strptime(mailed_dttm,"%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S")
| eval breach_discovered=strptime(breach_discovered_dttm,"%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S")
| eval days_to_mail=round((mailed_epoch-breach_discovered)/86400,1)
| where letter_status!="Mailed" OR days_to_mail>60 OR isnull(mail_tracking_id)
| stats count by incident_id, letter_status
| sort - count
description: Tracks generation, print, and certified mail tracking numbers for breach letters within the 60-day window to support individual notification compliance.
value: Gives the responsible team auditor-ready evidence for HIPAA Security compliance by automating regulatory compliance evidence using indexes privacy and sourcetypes hipaa:breach_notice, hipaa:breach_notice`. Replaces manual evidence gathering with a continuous Splunk search that produces structured, time-stamped artefacts suitable for regulatory examination and internal audit review.
implementation: (1) Integrate print vendor webhooks; (2) never index full SSN—use tokenized IDs; (3) legal sign-off field in same event. Indexes required: `index=privacy`; Sourcetypes: `sourcetype=hipaa:breach_notice`, `sourcetype=hipaa:breach_notice``.
visualization: Gantt-style timeline (discovered → mailed), Table (exceptions). Key fields: `breach_discovered`, `days_to_mail`, `incident_id`, `letter_status`, `mailed_epoch`.
knownFalsePositives:
1. **Break-the-glass emergency access** — Clinicians exercising documented emergency access procedures to ePHI during patient safety events generate elevated access alerts that are legitimate under the HIPAA treatment, payment, and operations (TPO) exception.

2. **Health information exchange (HIE) activity** — Automated ePHI transmissions through approved health information exchanges and interoperability interfaces produce cross-boundary data flows that are authorised under business associate agreements.

3. **Clinical research data extraction** — IRB-approved research protocols involve de-identified or limited data set extractions that trigger data access monitoring rules but are covered by the approved research data use agreement.

4. **System backup and archival processes** — Scheduled ePHI backup operations, media rotation, and long-term archival procedures generate bulk data access events that are operationally required and documented in the information lifecycle policy.

**Suppression mechanism:** Maintain a `hipaa_exception_register` KV Store lookup containing approved break-glass event IDs, HIE partner identifiers, IRB protocol numbers, and scheduled backup job identifiers. The correlation search filters against this lookup and suppresses matches where `hipaa_exception_approved=true`.
detailedImplementation:
## Prerequisites

• **Splunk**: Enterprise ≥9.2 or Splunk Cloud (current). Scheduling requires a search head not under memory pressure (check `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd component=ProcessRunner` for OOM warnings before deploying a new scheduled search).
• **Indexes**: `privacy` must exist on all indexers with retention matching the regulation's minimum (PCI: 1y, HIPAA: 6y, NIST 800-53: 1-3y per FIPS 199, SOX: 7y). Set `frozenTimePeriodInSecs` accordingly in `indexes.conf`.
• **Regulatory mapping**: This detection evidences clause `§164.404(b)`. The control objective is: "Evidence that HIPAA Security §164.404(b) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.10.45: Individual Notification — Letter Generation & Mailing Evidence." — every alert payload should include this text so downstream consumers know the compliance purpose.

## Step 1 — Configure data collection

Data collection is bespoke for this UC — no single canonical TA parses every source the detection relies on. Route data into `privacy` under `hipaa:breach_notice`, and document the ingestion path in the UC runbook so future engineers know where each field originates.


Three operational considerations, authored alongside this UC, apply:


• **Hint 1:** Integrate print vendor webhooks. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 2:** never index full SSN—use tokenized IDs. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 3:** legal sign-off field in same event. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

## Step 2 — Implement the search

For "Individual Notification — Letter Generation & Mailing Evidence," the aggregation is the evidence artifact itself — auditors will read these rows as the official record of HIPAA Security control activity.

This detection correlates event arrival time with a business timestamp from `mailed_dttm` (format: `%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S`). The `strptime()` conversion to epoch is the linchpin — the subsequent `where` clause compares Splunk's `_time` against the parsed epoch to detect policy violations (activity after a termination, events past a retention boundary, operations outside a maintenance window, etc).

Silent failure mode: if `mailed_dttm` arrives in a format the `strptime` call doesn't match, the conversion returns null, propagates through the `where` comparison as 'unknown,' and the row is dropped from results. This produces under-reporting, not over-reporting — which is the dangerous direction for a compliance detection. Validate by running:

```spl
<base search>
| eval mailed_dttm_epoch = strptime(mailed_dttm, "%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S")
| where isnull(mailed_dttm_epoch)
| stats count by mailed_dttm
```

If this returns rows, the upstream system has multiple timestamp formats (common with SaaS connectors that change during version upgrades) and the `strptime` call must be hardened with `coalesce(strptime(mailed_dttm, "fmt1"), strptime(mailed_dttm, "fmt2"))` to handle them all.

Time-zone alignment: if the source system emits `mailed_dttm` in a different zone from the Splunk search head, the comparison will be offset. Look for `%z` or `%Z` in the format string — if absent, the conversion uses `TZ = TZ of Splunk host`, which is correct only when source and target agree. Search covers the last 120 days.

**Schedule configuration** (`savedsearches.conf`):
```ini
[UC-22.10.45: Individual Notification — Letter Generation & Mailing Eviden]
cron_schedule = 0 */6 * * *
dispatch.earliest_time = -120d
dispatch.latest_time = now
actions = summary_index
action.summary_index._name = summary_compliance
action.summary_index.marker = uc=22.10.45,reg=HIPAA_Security
```

## Step 3 — Validate deployment

**(a) Field extraction check:** Run the following to confirm the TA is extracting every field the detection relies on:

```spl
index=privacy sourcetype=hipaa:breach_notice earliest=-1h
| fieldsummary
| where count=0
| table field
```

Any row returned is a field the TA claims to extract but isn't appearing in recent data — it's either a rename upstream, a TA version mismatch, or a data-format change.

**(b) Required-field presence:** This detection specifically needs: `breach_discovered`, `days_to_mail`, `incident_id`, `letter_status`, `mailed_epoch`. Run:

```spl
index=privacy sourcetype=hipaa:breach_notice earliest=-1h
| head 100
| table breach_discovered, days_to_mail, incident_id, letter_status, mailed_epoch
```

Every row should have non-null values for the fields the detection's `where` clause filters on. Nulls cause silent drops in the result set.

**(d) Threshold calibration:** Before enabling as a production alert, run the base search with the threshold removed and profile the distribution of `days_to_mail`:

```spl
<base search> earliest=-30d
| stats perc50(days_to_mail) as p50 perc90(days_to_mail) as p90 perc99(days_to_mail) as p99 max(days_to_mail) as hi
```

The current threshold `60` should sit between p90 and p99 for a balanced signal-to-noise ratio. If it's below p50, expect heavy alert volume; if above p99, expect under-detection.

**(e) Negative test:** Generate a synthetic 'should not alert' event using the Splunk Sample Generator or a crafted `| makeresults` event and confirm the detection does NOT fire on it. A detection that fires on everything is equivalent to a detection that fires on nothing.

## Step 4 — Operationalize (dashboard and reporting)

Create a companion dashboard titled `UC-22.10.45: HIPAA Security evidence` with the following panel layout:

**Panel 1 — Gantt-Style Timeline (Discovered → Mailed) (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.

**Panel 2 — Drill-through table (`exceptions`):** Full-width bottom row. Enable row-level drilldown to a secondary dashboard that expands each violation with its raw events, timeline, and related context. This is the panel the auditor will actually read — optimise it for scannability (avoid huge wrap-around fields).


Time picker defaults: set to last 30 days with named presets for 24h, 7d, 30d, 90d, and 1y — the auditor needs all five windows without retyping. Enable scheduled PDF export for the full dashboard on the regulation's reporting cadence.

## Step 5 — Troubleshooting and regulation-specific caveats

**Silent under-reporting:** The `strptime` conversion is likely failing on a subset of events — see Step 3 for the hardening procedure. Check for format drift after upstream system upgrades.

**Search times out:** Check the search job inspector (`Activity > Jobs > <this UC's last run> > Inspect`). The phase consuming the time (fetch, command, summary) indicates whether the issue is data-layer (too wide a window, unaccelerated datamodel), command-layer (expensive `transaction` or `eventstats`), or post-aggregation.


**HIPAA Security-specific failure modes:**

• Minimum-necessary principle: access-anomaly detections (excessive chart views, unexpected departments) must NOT flag clinicians accessing records for treatment purposes — §164.502(b) explicitly permits this. Join detections against the clinician's current department and patient's active encounter to avoid generating thousands of false positives on ED doctors seeing admitted patients.

• Break-glass and emergency access (§164.312(a)(2)(ii)): emergency accounts will legitimately bypass normal access controls during downtime events. Maintain a lookup `breakglass_declarations.csv` written by the clinical informatics on-call; correlate every break-glass access against a declared event — un-declared emergency access is the finding.

• Audit log retention (§164.312(b)): HIPAA requires 6 years of audit log retention, longer than most Splunk index default settings. Verify `frozenTimePeriodInSecs >= 189216000` for every index named in an HIPAA UC's search, and that the archive tier (S3, cold storage) is provisioned.

• Covered vs non-covered entity scope: if the search unintentionally pulls data from a non-covered business unit (e.g. a research arm not operating as a HIPAA Covered Entity), findings are noise. Filter using the facility/cost-center lookup that the Privacy Officer maintains, not by index name alone.
grandmaExplanation: We track generation, print, and certified mail tracking numbers for breach letters within the 60-day window to support individual notification compliance. HIPAA is about keeping health records private and secure when we use them in care and operations.
